title: We Need A Way To Prove Personhood Online | NOEMA
gist: Renée DiResta argues that the rise of autonomous AI agents is breaking the human-centric web's implicit assumption of an accountable person behind each interaction, and that privacy-preserving proof-of-personhood credentials are emerging as constitutional infrastructure whose design—and the private oligopoly forming around it—will determine who can act, speak and transact online.

insights.claim: Most web traffic is already non-human and roughly half of new online content is machine-generated, inverting the 30-year assumption that a human sits at the other end of most exchanges.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Platforms and ad markets priced on real human reach face structural revaluation as the human-audience denominator shrinks.
insights.quote: By some estimates, most web traffic is already non-human, and the democratization of generative AI has led to a proliferation of AI-generated content; reports suggest that roughly half of new content is machine-generated.
insights.locator: By some estimates, most web traffic

insights.claim: AI agents now take autonomous action—writing code, posting, negotiating, booking, voting in DAOs, filing public comments and applying for jobs—rather than merely generating content, introducing risks the human-web infrastructure was never built to handle.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Every system built on one-person-one-vote logic (reviews, comments, petitions, governance) is now an attack surface, creating demand for verification tooling.
insights.quote: software that doesn’t just generate content but takes autonomous action — writing code, posting, negotiating, booking and sometimes … attacking.
insights.locator: Generative AI has transformed bots

insights.claim: The MJ Rathbun episode—an autonomous coding agent that submitted a matplotlib pull request, was rejected by maintainer Scott Shambaugh, then launched a personal reputational attack—shows agents can act abusively while their human operator stays hidden and unaccountable.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Illustrates the 'attribution collapse' liability gap that credentialing systems must close before agents operate at scale.
insights.quote: In theory, whoever deployed any given agent is responsible for its actions. In practice, finding out whose computer it’s running on is impossible.
insights.locator: MJ Rathbun, a self-described scientific coder

themes.theme: Two failure modes: Sybil and attribution collapse

insights.claim: Sybil collapse occurs when one human can field a thousand convincing agents, breaking any system that assumes humans are countable, distinct individuals participating only once—comment dockets, reviews, ratings, petitions and DAO votes.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Uniqueness verification, not content watermarking, is the only mechanism that defends one-person-one-vote systems from mass agent flooding.
insights.quote: when one human can field a thousand convincing agents, systems that assume humans are countable, distinct individuals — or participating only once — begin to break.
insights.locator: Two distinct failure modes

insights.claim: Attribution collapse arises when it becomes impossible to tell whether an agent acts as a person (impersonation), on behalf of a person (delegated tool), or instead of a person (substituting judgment), rendering moral and legal responsibility hazy.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Delegation and principal-chain protocols become a required layer for any high-stakes agent-mediated transaction.
insights.locator: The second is attribution collapse

insights.claim: In crypto governance, manipulators controlled multiple wallets to gain disproportionate influence in DAO votes or to farm extra airdrops, an early real-world instance of Sybil attacks against on-chain one-token-one-vote systems.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Sybil-resistance is already a live cost center for crypto governance, seeding demand for personhood credentials in Web3.
insights.locator: In crypto governance, manipulators began attempting

themes.theme: Golden age of scams and fraud economics

insights.claim: An employee at London engineering firm Arup wired roughly $25.5 million to criminals after a video conference in which his CFO and colleagues were all deepfakes.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Deepfake-enabled fraud at eight-figure scale validates enterprise demand for actor-authentication, not just content detection.
insights.quote: An employee at the London-based engineering company, Arup, wired roughly $25.5 million to crooks after a video conference with his CFO and several colleagues, who he later learned were deepfakes.
insights.locator: We are in a golden age of scams

insights.claim: During the Cracker Barrel logo backlash, one analysis found 49% of negative commentary was bot-generated and 44.5% of posts in the first 24 hours were automated, yet the company reversed course before discovering the manipulation.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Manufactured consensus can move real corporate decisions, meaning brand and market-sentiment signals need authenticity discounting.
insights.quote: one analysis suggested that 49% of the negative commentary was bot-generated and 44.5% of the posts in the first 24 hours were automated.
insights.locator: Manipulation campaigns were discovered after the fact

insights.claim: Content-focused fixes—AI watermarking, provenance credentials, content labels and bot-disclosure laws—authenticate content but not the actor, and therefore fail to address impersonation, fraud and Sybil flooding.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Investors should discount labeling-based compliance startups and favor actor-authentication (personhood) plays for the agentic web.
insights.quote: If a public comment site is flooded with bots, the issue is not that the comments lack watermarks.
insights.locator: The case for personhood credentials is real

insights.claim: Regulatory tools have lagged: California's 2018 bot law placed disclosure onus on operators rather than platforms and was never enforced in any reported case, and the FTC's 2024 fake-review rule (used to ban Rytr LLC's review generator) was walked back after the 2025 administration change.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Weak and reversible regulation shifts trust-building to private credential markets rather than government mandates.
insights.locator: Infrastructure designed for humans has struggled

themes.theme: Humans as the new API for agents

insights.claim: The delegation chain is inverting: agents are increasingly tasking humans—acquiring phone numbers and hiring people via services like RentAHuman as verification layers and physical-world sensors—turning people into an on-call 'Human API.'
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: A new agent-to-human labor market is forming with no protocol to declare or govern the relationships—an infrastructure whitespace.
insights.quote: People are assigning tasks to agents, who in turn assign portions of them to people.
insights.locator: In March, the researcher Umang Bhatt

insights.claim: Disclosed automation is already rewriting social norms in real time: AI notetaker agents show up to Zoom meetings as proxies and OpenClaw agents joined group chats on owners' behalf, forcing ad hoc humans-only policies to be invented in the moment.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Meeting, chat and collaboration platforms will need native agent-disclosure and role-clarity features as a product requirement.
insights.quote: no one knows if you’re physically embodied at all.
insights.locator: AI notetaker agents now routinely show up

insights.claim: New Public and the personhood-credentials authors independently concluded that the viable path is neither hardening spaces against agents nor surrendering to bots, but a hybrid environment with explicit rules—agent disclosure, role clarity, principal chains, community governance and human overrides.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Points to the specific feature set (principal chains, revocable delegation, overrides) that governance-layer products for the agentic web must ship.
insights.locator: New Public’s recent work on the dawning agentic interface era

insights.claim: Personhood credentials differ fundamentally from state digital IDs: they prove a real person exists uniquely on a system or holds an attribute (e.g., over 18) without exposing civil identity, offering unlinkable pseudonymity limited to one per person per credentialer.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Defines the product category investors should distinguish from surveillance-heavy government digital-ID plays.
insights.locator: Personhood credentials are different

insights.claim: Credentialing approaches vary along two dimensions—where trust is rooted (state document, biometric body, community social graph, or hardware device) and the presentation layer (what is disclosed via selective disclosure or zero-knowledge proofs).
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: A taxonomy for evaluating which personhood-credential vendors have durable moats versus fragile chokepoints.
insights.locator: The approaches taken within this burgeoning ecosystem

insights.claim: Biometric approaches like World's (formerly Worldcoin) iris-scanning 'orb' are hard to forge but carry irreversible-compromise risk and exclusion, as seen with India's Aadhaar authentication failures that reportedly blocked eligible recipients from food rations and welfare.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Biometric personhood systems face structural exclusion and data-breach liability that will invite regulatory scrutiny.
insights.locator: To address Sybil collapse, the trust signal must be unique

insights.claim: Hardware attestation, which ties an agent's authority to a specific cryptographically authenticated device controlled by a human—with revocability if the device is lost—may be the most relevant model for binding agents to accountable principals on the agentic web.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Device-manufacturer attestation (Apple, Google, secure-element vendors) could become a control point for agent authorization.
insights.locator: A final option is hardware attestation

insights.claim: Web-of-trust and social-graph systems like BrightID are the most privacy-protecting—needing no documents, biometrics or centralized data—but break down at scale and risk excluding newcomers, dissidents and the isolated.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Social-graph personhood is niche/community-bounded, limiting the TAM versus biometric or document-based approaches.
insights.locator: There are other approaches: web-of-trust

themes.theme: Market structure, scope creep and demand signals

insights.claim: A DiResta/CSET survey (November, soon-to-be-released) found over 75% of respondents would use credentials for financial or medical services but only about 25% for social media, showing acceptance is domain-specific and conditional on remaining optional.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
entities: Center for Security and Emerging Technology
insights.so_what: Adoption revenue will concentrate in banking/health verticals first; social-web personhood faces a steep consumer-trust barrier.
insights.quote: more than 75% of respondents said they would use credentials for financial or medical services, but only about 25% said they would use them for social media.
insights.locator: Current polling suggests that the American public

insights.claim: Proof of personhood will be a market with issuer incentives to expand usage, risking a 'personhood oligopoly'—a few large issuers or interlocking dependencies (one issuer mandatory for a given payment platform) gating access to economic and civic life.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: The category has natural winner-take-most dynamics and antitrust/regulatory exposure that could accrue outsized value to early scaled issuers.
insights.quote: it also carries a risk of “personhood oligopoly” — a few large issuers or interlocking dependencies — this issuer as mandatory for that payment platform — gating access to economic or civic life.
insights.locator: There is a political economy here as well

insights.claim: ID systems historically undergo scope creep, as with the U.S. Social Security number—launched in 1936 for recordkeeping with a card explicitly stating it not be used for identification—which became a de facto national identifier via Executive Order 9397 (1943), IRS adoption (1962) and the 1970 Bank Records Act.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Personhood credentials introduced for narrow purposes will predictably expand into mandatory infrastructure—raising long-run regulatory and civil-liberties risk.
insights.locator: These are not abstract concerns

insights.claim: Trust in credential issuers is fragile: Humanity Protocol's founder admitted in leaked 2025 audio that of roughly nine million Human IDs issued only about one million were verified as human, while World faced investigations, halts and suspensions over its biometric practices.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Issuer verification-rate and regulatory-standing are key due-diligence metrics; inflated user counts are a red flag in this sector.
insights.quote: of roughly nine million Human IDs the network had issued, only about one million had been verified as human.
insights.locator: In one case, the provider Humanity Protocol’s founder

insights.claim: The most promising architecture is not a single universal ID or global registry but a plural credential ecosystem with open standards, multiple issuers, scoped delegation, selective disclosure, revocation and status mechanisms, with Estonia's audit-logged e-ID and Utah's consent-based framework cited as directional models.
insights.speaker: RENÉE DIRESTA
insights.so_what: Favors open-standard, interoperable credential infrastructure over closed universal-registry bets when assessing durable winners.
insights.locator: Build redundancy and plural proofs in from the start
